topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the lines of the manuscript with a fingertip, the ink cool beneath his touch. Every carefully constructed sentence, every evocative turn of phrase, felt suddenly hollow. He reread the passage, the familiar rhythm of the narrative now grating against him like sandpaper. A cold prickle crawled up his neck, and he instinctively pulled his hand back, wiping his palm on his trousers as if to dislodge something clinging there. He had poured months of his soul into those characters, those worlds, and now they felt borrowed, cheap. He leaned back in his chair, the squeak of the worn leather protesting his movement, and stared at the ceiling, the silence of his study amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest.

[story 2]

"This isn't right," Clara murmured, her voice barely audible above the distant hum of city traffic. She kept glancing over her shoulder at the screen, the words shimmering on the monitor mocking her. She picked up her coffee mug, the ceramic warm against her chilled fingers, and turned it slowly in her hands, focusing intensely on the swirling brown liquid. Her jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath her skin. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of her apartment, the floorboards creaking under her weight with every hurried step. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating.

[story 3]

When the email arrived, Julian didn't immediately open it. He stared at the notification banner, the stark white text seeming to vibrate on the dark screen. A tremor ran through his hands, causing him to grip the edge of his desk until his knuckles bleached white. He reread the correspondence three times, his eyes darting back and forth across the text, searching for a flaw, a misplaced comma, anything to justify the jarring feeling settling deep in his stomach. He pushed his chair back, the scrape echoing loudly in the quiet office, and walked to the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, feeling profoundly unsettled by the sudden intrusion.
